assignments.title: Set Theory Fundamentals
assignments.description: For this assignment, you will provide a written response to the following questions: Define the basic set operations, including union, intersection, and difference. Explain the concept of a power set and how to calculate it for a given set. Provide examples of set theory applications in real-world scenarios. Your response should be well-organized, clearly written, and approximately 500-750 words in length.
requirements: Define and explain the basic set operations (union, intersection, difference).
Describe the concept of a power set and provide a step-by-step example of how to calculate it for a given set.
Provide at least two real-world examples of set theory applications.
Ensure the response is 500-750 words in length.
Use proper mathematical notation and terminology.

assignments.title: Set Theory Operations
assignments.description: For this quiz, students will demonstrate their understanding of set theory operations, including union, intersection, difference, and complement. The quiz will consist of multiple-choice questions and short-answer problems that require students to apply set theory concepts to solve problems.
requirements: Complete 10 multiple-choice questions on set theory operations within 30 minutes.
Solve 2 short-answer problems that require students to show their work and explain their reasoning.
Achieve a minimum score of 70% to pass the quiz.

assignments.title: Basic Mathematical Structures
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will review and familiarize themselves with basic mathematical structures. This includes understanding sets, relations, and functions, which form the foundation of discrete mathematics. Students are expected to identify and list the key properties and types of these structures.
requirements: Read sections 1.1-1.3 in the textbook 'Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications' by Kenneth H. Rosen.
List and define the key types of sets (e.g., empty set, finite set, infinite set).
Identify and describe the properties of relations (e.g., reflexive, symmetric, transitive).
Provide examples of different types of functions (e.g., one-to-one, onto, bijective).

Submit a checklist with the following items:
            - Definition of a set
            - Properties of relations
            - Types of functions
            - Examples of each

assignments.title: Relations and Functions
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will demonstrate their understanding of relations and functions in discrete mathematics. Students are expected to provide clear and concise written responses to the following prompts: Define what a relation is and provide examples of relations in real-world scenarios. Explain the properties of relations, including reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity. Describe the concept of a function, including the definition of domain and range. Provide examples of functions and explain how they differ from relations. Use mathematical notation to represent relations and functions.
requirements: The submission should be in a written format (e.g., PDF, Word document, or text file).
The submission should include a clear and concise definition of a relation and its properties.
The submission should include examples of relations and functions in real-world scenarios.
The submission should use proper mathematical notation to represent relations and functions.
The submission should be 2-3 pages in length, double-spaced.

assignments.title: Relations Properties
assignments.description: For this quiz, students will demonstrate their understanding of the properties of relations, including reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity. Students will be given a set of relations and asked to identify which properties each relation satisfies. The quiz will consist of multiple-choice questions and short-answer questions that require students to provide explanations for their answers.
requirements: Read sections 6.2-6.4 in the textbook, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications by Kenneth H. Rosen.
Complete the provided practice problems on relations properties before taking the quiz.
Answer all multiple-choice questions correctly.
Provide clear and concise explanations for short-answer questions, demonstrating understanding of reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.

assignments.title: Graph Theory Basics
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will provide a written response to the topic of graph theory basics. Students should review the relevant chapters in the textbook, specifically focusing on the definition and properties of graphs, including vertices, edges, and adjacency. The response should include a clear explanation of these concepts, along with examples to illustrate their understanding. Students are expected to use proper mathematical notation and terminology throughout their response.
requirements: A clear and concise definition of a graph, including its components (vertices, edges).
An explanation of the concept of adjacency in graphs, with examples.
Discussion of at least two types of graphs (e.g., simple graphs, weighted graphs) and their characteristics.
Proper use of mathematical notation and terminology related to graph theory.
Examples or diagrams to support explanations.

assignments.title: Graph Structures
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will demonstrate their understanding of various graph structures by identifying and describing key properties. Students should review the relevant sections in the textbook, specifically focusing on graph terminology, types of graphs (such as simple graphs, multigraphs, pseudographs, directed graphs, and weighted graphs), and common graph structures like complete graphs, cycles, and wheels. Students will then complete a checklist that requires them to correctly classify and describe different graph structures based on their properties.
requirements: Identify and describe the properties of simple graphs, multigraphs, pseudographs, directed graphs, and weighted graphs.
Recognize and explain the characteristics of complete graphs, cycles, wheels, and other common graph structures.
Classify given graph structures into their respective categories.
Provide accurate descriptions of the properties and features of each graph structure type.

assignments.title: Combinatorics Fundamentals
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will demonstrate their understanding of combinatorics fundamentals by solving a series of problems related to counting principles, permutations, and combinations. Students should provide detailed solutions to each problem, showing all necessary steps and calculations. The goal is to apply mathematical concepts to solve real-world problems and develop problem-solving skills.
requirements: Read sections 5.1-5.3 in the textbook 'Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications' by Kenneth H. Rosen.
Complete problems 1-10 on page 125 of the textbook, showing all work and calculations.

Solve the following additional problems: If a set contains 10 elements, how many subsets does it have? A committee of 3 people is to be formed from a group of 10 people. How many different committees are possible?
Submit your solutions as a single PDF document, with each problem clearly labeled and numbered.

assignments.title: Permutations and Combinations
assignments.description: In this quiz, students will demonstrate their understanding of permutations and combinations. The quiz will consist of multiple-choice questions and problems that require students to calculate permutations and combinations in various scenarios. Students will need to apply formulas and concepts learned in the course to solve problems.
requirements: Students must complete all questions within the given time limit.
Students must show all work and calculations for each problem.
Students must achieve a minimum score of 70% to pass the quiz.

assignments.title: Propositional Logic
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will demonstrate an understanding of propositional logic by providing detailed solutions to a set of problems. The goal is to apply the concepts of propositional logic to evaluate and simplify logical expressions, and to construct truth tables for given propositions.
requirements: Read sections 1.4 and 1.5 of the textbook, 'Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications' by Kenneth H. Rosen.

Complete the following problems:
      1. Let p, q, and r be the propositions:
         p: You have the flu.
         q: You miss the final examination.
         r: You pass the course.
         Express the following propositions as logical expressions:
         a) You have the flu and miss the final examination.
         b) You have the flu and pass the course.
         c) You miss the final examination and pass the course.
      2. Construct truth tables for the following propositions:
         a) (p ∧ q) → r
         b) (p ∨ q) ↔ r
      3. Determine whether the following propositions are tautologies:
         a) (p ∧ q) → (p ∨ q)
         b) (p → q) ↔ (¬q → ¬p)
Submit a written document with clear explanations and justifications for all answers.
Include your name, course number, week number, and assignment title at the top of the page.

assignments.title: Predicate Logic
assignments.description: For this quiz on Predicate Logic, students will need to understand the concepts of predicates, quantifiers (universal and existential), and how to apply them to express statements in a formal and precise manner. The quiz will test their ability to interpret and construct predicate logic expressions, as well as reason about the truth values of statements involving predicates and quantifiers.
requirements: Read sections 1.4 (Predicate Logic) in the textbook.
Complete the provided practice problems on predicate logic expressions and quantifiers.
Be prepared to define and explain key terms such as predicate, domain, universal quantifier, and existential quantifier.
Work through sample quiz questions that illustrate the application of predicate logic in expressing and evaluating statements.

assignments.title: Mathematical Proofs
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will write a detailed mathematical proof for a given problem. The goal is to apply logical reasoning and mathematical principles to derive a valid conclusion. Students should carefully read the problem statement, identify the premises and the conclusion to be proven, and then construct a step-by-step proof using appropriate mathematical notation and logical rules.
requirements: The proof should start with a clear statement of what is to be proven.
Each step in the proof must be justified by a relevant mathematical principle or previously established fact.
The proof should conclude with a clear statement of the proven result.
Students must use proper mathematical notation and terminology throughout the proof.
The assignment should be submitted in a clear and legible format, either typed or handwritten with clear scans or photographs.

assignments.title: Problem-Solving
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will complete a checklist to demonstrate their understanding of problem-solving strategies in discrete mathematics. The checklist will cover various problem-solving techniques learned throughout the course, including but not limited to, analyzing problem statements, identifying key concepts, and applying relevant theorems and algorithms.
requirements: Complete a checklist of 10 problem-solving strategies in discrete mathematics.
For each strategy, provide a brief description (1-2 sentences) of how it can be applied to solve problems in discrete mathematics.
Ensure that all checklist items are accurately completed and submitted on time.

assignments.title: Graph Algorithms
assignments.description: For this written assignment, you will explore and describe various graph algorithms. Using the concepts learned in class and the textbook 'Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications' by Kenneth H. Rosen, you are expected to provide a detailed explanation of at least three fundamental graph algorithms: Breadth-First Search (BFS), Depth-First Search (DFS), and Dijkstra's algorithm. Your response should include a clear description of each algorithm, their applications, and examples of how they are used in real-world scenarios. Make sure to use proper mathematical notation and provide any necessary diagrams to support your explanations.
requirements: Provide a detailed description of the Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm, including its steps and an example of its application.
Explain the Depth-First Search (DFS) algorithm, including its steps and an example of its application.
Describe Dijkstra's algorithm for finding the shortest path in a graph, including its steps and an example of its application.
Use proper mathematical notation and include any necessary diagrams to support your explanations.
Ensure your work is well-organized and clearly written.

assignments.title: Graph Traversals
assignments.description: In this quiz, students will demonstrate their understanding of graph traversals, including depth-first search (DFS) and breadth-first search (BFS) algorithms. Students will be given a set of graph structures and asked to identify the correct traversal order for each graph.
requirements: Read sections 10.3 and 10.4 in the textbook, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications by Kenneth H. Rosen.
Complete the provided practice problems on graph traversals.
Take the quiz within the given time frame and submit it through the course platform.

assignments.title: Course Review
assignments.description: For this course review assignment, students will verify their understanding of key concepts covered throughout the course. The task involves reviewing all the major topics in discrete mathematics, including set theory, relations, functions, graph theory, combinatorics, propositional and predicate logic, and mathematical proofs.
requirements: Review and summarize the main concepts from weeks 1 through 7.
Ensure understanding of set theory, relations, functions, graph theory, combinatorics, and logic.
Complete a checklist of key terms and concepts to confirm familiarity.
Submit the completed checklist for review.

assignments.title: Practice Problems
assignments.description: For this assignment, students will work on a variety of practice problems that cover all the topics studied throughout the course. The goal is to reinforce understanding and problem-solving skills in discrete mathematics. Students should review all previous material and attempt to solve problems from each week's topics. The submission should include detailed solutions to the problems, clearly showing the steps taken to reach the answers.
requirements: Complete a minimum of 20 practice problems covering at least 5 different topics from the course.
Provide detailed solutions to each problem, including all steps and explanations.
Organize solutions in a clear and readable format, using proper mathematical notation and terminology.
Submit solutions in a single document, either in PDF or Word format.
